title: practical english usage - Part 132
content:
Much  has been written about unemployment. In the opinion of many In a formal style, much  and many are more common in affirmative clauses.

'Didyou buy any clothes?' 'Yes, lots.' (     Yes, many.) (More natural than He's got many men friends ...) He's got lots of men friends, but he doesn't know many women.

'How much  money have you got?1 'I've got plenty.' (     Fve-getrmueh/) other words and expressions are used instead.

clauses. In most informal affirmative clauses they are unusual (especially much); In an informal style, we  use much  and many mostly in questions and negative 4 not   used    in  affirmative        clauses Many people think it's time for a change. (More natural than Many think...) Many is not usually used alone to mean ’ many people’ .

dropped. You couldn’ t say The food wasn't much-food.) but not There wasn't much  (food), (           The food-wasn't mu ch? No noun has been dropped.

Note that much  and many are only used like this when a noun has been 'Did you find any mushrooms?' ’ Not many.' You haven't eaten much.

We   can drop a noun after much  or many, if the meaning is clear.

3 much/many            without      a  noun Not much  of Denmark is hilly.

I've seen too much  of Howard recently.

However, much  of can be used directly before personal and geographical names.

There aren't many of them left.

There aren't many of the large glasses left.

- There aren 't many large glasses left.

She didn't eat much  of it.

She didn't eat much  of her breakfast.

- She didn't eat much  breakfast, (     . . .-mueh of breakfast.) pronoun. Compare: We   use much/many of before another determiner (article, possessive, etc) or a 2  much/many            of I haven't got much  time. I don't know many of your friends.

Much  is used with singular (uncountable) nouns; many is used with plurals.

165  much      and   many grammar •  166   more There's just one more river to cross.

before a countable noun.

Note the structure one more, two more, etc. In this case more can be used 3 one     more,      etc I'd like some more, please.

We   can drop a noun after more if the meaning is clear.

2  more      without      a  noun Five hundred years ago, much  more of Britain was covered with trees.

It would be nice to see more of Ray and Barbara.

However, more of can be used directly before personal and geographical names.

More of them have been found.

More of the missing climbers have been found.

- More climbers have been found.

We need more of it.

We need more of that salted butter.

- We need more butter.

another determiner (article, possessive, etc) or a pronoun, we  use more of We   can use more as a determiner before uncountable or plural nouns. Before 1 more      (of) 166 more For much  and very with past participles (e.g. much/very amused), ►    96.8 I very much  like your new hairstyle.  Thank you very much.

Very much  can be used in affirmative clauses as an adverb.

I didn't much  enjoy the concert.

I much  appreciate your help. We much  prefer the country to the town.

in a formal style.

similar ideas, in affirmative clauses as well as questions and negatives, especially Much  can be used before some verbs expressing enjoyment, preference and She's much  older than her brother. I don't drive much  faster than you.

clauses as well as questions and negatives.

We   can also use much  before comparative adjectives and adverbs, in affirmative Do you work much  at weekends? I don't travel much  these days.

We   can use much  as an adverb in questions and negative clauses.

7 much      as  adverb her many prizes. This is rather formal.

Note that many can follow possessives in expressions like my many friends, 6 my    many       friends,        etc I play as much  tennis as I can. You make too many mistakes.

There was so much  traffic that it took me an hour to get home.

affirmative clauses.
